Ayahs

تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١)
Blessed is He in whose hands is all sovereignty, and He has power over all things. (1)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَٱلْحَيَوٰةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفُورُ (٢)
He, who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving. (2)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا ۖ مَّا تَرَىٰ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مِن تَفَـٰوُتٍ ۖ فَٱرْجِعِ ٱلْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرَىٰ مِن فُطُورٍ (٣)
He created seven heavens in layers. You do not see any discordance in the creation of the All-beneficent. Look again! Do you see any flaw? (3)
ثُمَّ ٱرْجِعِ ٱلْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْبَصَرُ خَاسِئًا وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ (٤)
Look again, once more. Your look will return to you humbled and weary. (4)
وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا رُجُومًا لِّلشَّيَـٰطِينِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٥)
We have certainly adorned the lowest heaven with lamps, and made them [the means of pelting] missiles against the devils, and We have prepared for them a punishment of the Blaze. (5)

Content of Surah al-Mulk

This Surah, which marks the beginning of the 29th Juz’ of the Holy Quran, is among the Surahs that, according to the popular view, were entirely revealed in Mecca. Just as most Surahs in this Juz’ are Meccan—indeed, according to some commentators, all Surahs in this Juz’ are Meccan ^1—this stands in contrast to the Surahs of the previous Juz’, which were Medinan. However, as we shall mention:

Surah al-Dahr (or Surah al-Insan) is an exception to this rule and was revealed in Medina.

Surah "al-Mulk," whose other name is Surah "al-Munjiyah" (The Deliverer) and whose third name is "al-Waqiyah" or "al-Mani'ah" (The Protector or The Preventer—because it protects its reciter from Divine punishment or the punishment of the grave), is among the highly virtuous Surahs of the Quran. Many issues are raised within it, which mainly revolve around three axes:

  1. Discussions regarding the "Origin" (Mabda’) and the Attributes of Allah, and the wondrous system of creation, especially the creation of the heavens and the stars, the creation of the earth and its bounties, as well as the creation of birds, flowing waters, and the creation of hearing, sight, and the tools of cognition.

  2. Discussions regarding the "Resurrection" (Ma’ad) and the punishment of Hell, the dialogues between the guardians of punishment and the denizens of Hell, and the like.

^1: Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 8, p. 180.

  1. Warning and threatening the disbelievers and oppressors with various types of punishment in this world and the Hereafter.

According to some, from the perspective of the main axis, the entire Surah is formed by the Sovereignty and Governance of God, which is mentioned in the first verse. ^1

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

Many narrations regarding the virtue of this Surah have been related from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام:

Among them, we read in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whoever recites Surah 'Tabarak,' it is as if he has kept the vigil of Laylat al-Qadr!" [^2] And in another Hadith from him, it is stated: "I would love for 'Tabarak al-Mulk' to be recorded in the heart of every believer." [^3] In a Hadith from Imam Muhammad bin Ali al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Surah al-Mulk is the 'Mani'ah' (The Preventer); it prevents the punishment of the grave. It is recorded in the Torah as Surah al-Mulk. Whoever recites it in a night has indeed recited much and well, and shall not be recorded among the heedless." ^1 And the Hadiths in this regard are numerous.

Of course, all these great effects are not related to reciting without reflection and action; the goal is a recitation intertwined with drawing inspiration for action.

^1: Ibid, p. 184. [^2] and [^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 320.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ تَبارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ (1) الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَ الْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ (2) الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً ما تَرى‌ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمنِ مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرى‌ مِنْ فُطُورٍ (3) ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنْقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خاسِئاً وَ هُوَ حَسِيرٌ (4) وَ لَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ وَ جَعَلْناها رُجُوماً لِلشَّياطِينِ وَ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذابَ السَّعِيرِ (5)

Commentary

You See No Flaw in the Universe

This Surah begins with the important matter of the Ownership and Sovereignty of Allah and the eternity of His Holy Essence, which is in fact the key to all the discussions in this Surah. ^1 It says: "Blessed and Everlasting is He in Whose hand is the dominion of the universe, and He has power over all things" (تَبارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

^1: This is the second Surah that begins with the word "Tabarak"; the other was Surah al-Furqan, which began with the sentence: "تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً".

"Tabarak" is derived from the root "Barakah," originally from "Barak" (rhyming with barg), meaning the chest of a camel. When it is said "Baraka al-Ba'ir," it means the camel struck its chest upon the ground. Subsequently, this word came to mean permanence, endurance, and imperishability, and it is used for any blessing that is stable and lasting. A water reservoir is called "Birka" for this reason—because water remains in it for a long time.

In the aforementioned verse, the reason for the Blessed nature of God's Holy Essence is actually mentioned: His Ownership and Sovereignty over the world and His power over all things. For this reason, He is an imperishable and most Blessed Being.

In the following verse, it points to the purpose of the creation of human death and life, which is among the affairs of God's Ownership and Sovereignty, saying: "He is the One Who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct" (الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَ الْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا). If "death" meant annihilation and non-existence, it would not be "created," for creation pertains to existential matters. However, we know that the reality of death is a transition from one world to another, and this is certainly an existential matter that can be created.

And if death is mentioned here before life, it is because of the profound effect that mindfulness of death has on the excellence of conduct. Furthermore, death [in the state of non-existence] preceded life.

The purpose of Allah's "testing," as we have stated before, is a form of cultivation; meaning He draws human beings into the field of action so they may become seasoned, tested, pure, and refined, and become worthy of proximity to God. ^1 It is noteworthy that He introduced the goal as the "test of excellence" of conduct, not the "quantity" of conduct. This is proof that Islam gives importance to "quality," not "quantity." What matters is that the action be sincere, for God, useful, and comprehensive, even if it is small in terms of quantity.

Therefore, regarding what is meant by "أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا" (best in conduct), it is narrated in some Islamic traditions from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "It means: which of you is most complete in intellect, most intense in fear of Allah, and best in observing what Allah has commanded and forbidden, even if your voluntary [supererogatory] deeds are fewer!" [^2] Obviously, a complete intellect makes the action purer, the intention more sincere, and the reward greater.

And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "It does not mean who performs more deeds, but rather who performs them more correctly. And correct action is that which is accompanied by the fear of Allah and a sincere intention." Then he said: "Preserving the deed [from pollution] until it is sincere is harder than the deed itself. And the sincere righteous deed is that for which you do not want anyone to praise you except Allah, the Almighty and Majestic." ^1 Regarding the purpose of human creation, we had a detailed discussion in the commentary of Surah al-Dhariyat under verse 56: "وَ ما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ". There, the goal was mentioned as "servitude to God," and here as "the test of excellence of conduct." It is clear that the matter of testing and examination is not separate from the matter of servitude, just as the perfection of intellect, fear of God, and sincere intention—mentioned in the above narrations—constitute the spirit of servitude.

In this way, the world is a great field of testing for all human beings; the means of testing are death and life, and the goal of this great trial is reaching excellence of conduct, which means "the perfection of knowledge, sincerity of intention, and the performance of every good deed."

And if we see that some commentators have interpreted "أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا" here to mean remembering death, or preparing for death, and the like, it is in fact an allusion to instances of this general meaning.

And since in this great field of testing, man is afflicted with many slips, these slips should not make him despair or stop him from striving to reform himself. At the end of the verse, He gives the servants a promise of help and forgiveness, saying: "And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Forgiving" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ). Yes, He is capable of all things, and the Forgiver of every repentant person.

^1: For more explanation regarding Divine trials, please study Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 155 of Surah al-Baqarah. [^2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 322. ^1: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses in question.

After explaining the system of death and life, He addresses the general system of the world and invites man to study the entirety of the universe, so that through this, he may prepare himself for that great test. He says: "The same God Who created seven heavens one above another" (الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً). Regarding the "seven heavens," we discussed this shortly before in the commentary of verse 12 of Surah al-Talaq. Regarding "Tibaqan" here, we must add that based on this expression, the seven heavens are each situated above the other, for the meaning of "Mutabaqah" is originally to place something above another.

Now, if we consider the seven heavens as referring to the seven planets of the solar system that are visible to the naked eye, each has a specific distance from the sun and each is above the other. And if we consider all the fixed and planetary stars we see as part of the first heaven, it becomes clear that in higher stages there are other worlds, one situated above the other.

Following that, He adds: "You see no flaw, contradiction, or defect in the creation of the All-Beneficent" (ما تَرى‌ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمنِ مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ). With all the grandeur that the universe possesses, whatever exists is order, firmness, harmony, calculated compositions, and precise laws. If disorder were to find its way into any corner of the world, it would lead it to destruction.

From the wondrous and amazing system that governs a single atom and the particles of electrons and protons, to the systems governing the entire solar system, other systems, and galaxies—all are under the dominion of precise laws that drive them in a specific path. In short, everywhere is law and calculation, and everywhere is order and program.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He says: "Look again and observe the world with precision; do you see any rift, flaw, or discrepancy in the universe?" (فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرى‌ مِنْ فُطُورٍ). "Futur" is from the root "Fatr" (rhyming with satr), meaning to split lengthwise. It also means breaking (like Iftar of a fast) and disruption or corruption, and it carries this meaning in the verse under discussion. The point is that no matter how much man scrutinizes the world of creation, he will not see the slightest flaw or disproportion in it.

Therefore, in the next verse, to emphasize this same meaning, He adds: "Then look again, and look twice more at the universe; eventually your sight will return to you tired and weakened, having failed in its search for flaw or deficiency in this great world!" (ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنْقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خاسِئاً وَ هُوَ حَسِيرٌ). "Karratayn" is from the root "Karr" (rhyming with sharr), meaning attention and returning to something. "Karrah" means repetition and "Karratayn" is its dual form. However, some commentators have said that "Karratayn" here does not mean the literal dual, but rather repeated, successive, and numerous attentions.

Therefore, at the very least, the Quran in these verses commands people to look three times at the universe and study the secrets of creation—and in a sense, to be precise time and again—and when they are unable to find the slightest flaw or deficiency in this wondrous system, they become better acquainted with the Originator of this apparatus and the Creator of this world and His infinite Knowledge and Power.

"Khasi’" is from the root "Khasa’" and "Khusu’" (rhyming with madh and khushu’). When used regarding the eye, it means becoming tired and weakened, and when used regarding a "dog," it means driving it away. "Hasir" is from the root "Hasar" (rhyming with qasr), meaning to strip bare. Since man loses his strength and power when exhausted, as if stripped of his energies, it has come to mean fatigue and weakness. Therefore, "Khasi’" and "Hasir" in the above verse both carry a similar meaning and are for emphasis on the subject of the eye's helplessness and inability to see any deficiency in the system of the universe. Some have distinguished between the two as follows: "Khasi’" means frustrated and "Hasir" means powerless.

In any case, two important conclusions can be drawn from these verses: First, the Quran gives an emphatic command to all wayfarers of the path of truth to study and scrutinize the secrets of the universe and the wonders of creation as much as they can, and not to be content with once or twice. How many secrets there are that do not reveal themselves at the first or second glance; sharp eyes are needed to find them after several looks.

Second, the more precise and meticulous man becomes in this system, the better he perceives its harmony—a harmony free from any kind of defect, flaw, crookedness, or deviation. And if in a superficial and initial view, some phenomena of this world are seen as evils, pests, or corruption (such as earthquakes, floods, diseases, and unfortunate events that occasionally occur in human lives), in more precise studies, it becomes clear that they too have precise and important secrets and philosophies. ^1 These verses clearly point to the "Argument from Order," which says: the existence of order in any apparatus is a sign of the existence of knowledge and power behind that apparatus; otherwise, uncalculated accidental events and blind and deaf coincidences can never be the origin of order and calculation. As stated in the famous Hadith of Mufaddal from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Negligence never produces correctness, and contradiction does not become the origin of order." [^2]

The last verse under discussion casts a gaze upon the face of the sky and speaks of the bright and beautiful stars: It says: "We have adorned the lowest heaven with lamps [stars], and We have made them missiles for the devils, and We have prepared for them the punishment of the Blazing Fire" (وَ لَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ وَ جَعَلْناها رُجُوماً لِلشَّياطِينِ وَ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذابَ السَّعِيرِ). One look at the sky on a dark, starry night, and attention to those distant dreamlike worlds, and imagining the systems that govern them, and precision in the beauty, delicacy, grandeur, and the mysterious and majestic silence that overshadows them, enters man into a world full of gnosis and the light of Truth, and makes him journey through realms of the Lord's love that cannot be described by any tongue.

This verse once again emphasizes the fact that all the stars we see are all part of the first heaven—the heaven that is closest to us among the seven heavens—and for this reason, it is expressed as "al-Sama’ al-Dunya" (the near and lower heaven).

The expression "Rujum" (missiles) refers to meteors that are thrown like an arrow from one side of the sky to the other. We know that "meteors" are the remains of stars that have disintegrated during certain events; therefore, if it says We have made the celestial bodies missiles for the devils, it refers to these specific small stones.

As for how the devils are targeted by meteors—which are small wandering stones in the heavens—we have explained it in detail in Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 11, page 42 (under verse 18 of Surah al-Hijr) and in Volume 19, page 15 onwards (under verse 20 of Surah al-Saffat).

Key Point: The Grandeur of the World of Creation

Although the Holy Quran arose from the backward environment of the era of Arab ignorance (Jahiliyyah), it frequently emphasizes that Muslims should reflect upon the magnificent secrets of the universe—a matter that had no meaning in the era of ignorance. This itself is clear proof that the Quran was issued from another source, and the more science and knowledge progress, the grandeur of the Quran's emphases in this field becomes more apparent.

We know that the planet Earth on which we live, with all its greatness, is so small compared to the center of the solar system—the "Sun"—that if one million and two hundred thousand Earths were placed together, they would only then equal the size of the Sun! On the other hand, our solar system is part of a vast galaxy called the "Milky Way." ^1 According to the calculations of astronomers, in our galaxy alone there are over one hundred billion (100,000,000,000) stars, of which our Sun, with all its grandeur, is considered one of its average stars! Thirdly, in this great universe, there are so many galaxies that they are beyond calculation and numbering. And the more astronomical telescopes become larger and more equipped, new galaxies are discovered! How Great is the God Who has laid out this vast design with that precise system. All Grandeur belongs to Allah, the One, the Subduer.

^1: We have mentioned the explanation of this matter in the discussions on "Proving the Existence of God" in response to the arguments of materialists regarding "pests and calamities"; refer to the book The Creator of the World. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 3, p. 63. ^1: "Galaxies" are collections of stars known as "cities of stars," and although they are relatively close to one another, there are sometimes millions of light-years of distance between them!

Ayahs

وَلِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٦)
For those who defy their Lord is the punishment of hell, and it is an evil destination. (6)
إِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ فِيهَا سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا شَهِيقًا وَهِىَ تَفُورُ (٧)
When they are thrown in it, they hear it blaring, as it seethes, (7)
تَكَادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أُلْقِىَ فِيهَا فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَآ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ (٨)
almost exploding with rage. Whenever a group is thrown in it, its keepers will ask them, ‘Did not any warner come to you?’ (8)
قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَآءَنَا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنَا وَقُلْنَا مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ كَبِيرٍ (٩)
They will say, ‘Yes, a warner did come to us, but we impugned [him] and said, ‘Allah did not send down anything; you are only in great error.’ (9)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١٠)
And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among inmates of the Blaze.’ (10)
فَٱعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذَنۢبِهِمْ فَسُحْقًا لِّأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١١)
Thus they will admit their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze! (11)

Commentary

If We Had Listening Ears and Awakening Thought, We Would Not Be in Hell!

Since the previous verses spoke of the signs of Allah’s greatness and power and their proofs in the world of creation, the verses under discussion speak of those who ignore these proofs, choose the path of disbelief and polytheism, and like the devils, invite Divine punishment upon themselves.

First, He says: "And for those who disbelieve in their Lord is the punishment of Hell, and wretched is the destination" (وَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

Then, describing a part of this terrifying punishment, He adds: "When they are thrown into it, they hear from it a [horrible] inhaling while it boils up" (إِذا أُلْقُوا فِيها سَمِعُوا لَها شَهِيقاً وَ هِيَ تَفُورُ).

Yes, when they are cast into it with the utmost humiliation and insignificance, the terrifying and prolonged cry of Hell rises, plunging their entire being into terror.

"Shahiq" (شهيق) originally means an ugly and repulsive sound like the braying of a donkey, and it is sometimes said to be derived from the root "Shahuq" (شهوق) meaning to be long (hence a high mountain is called Jabal Shahiq); therefore, "Shahiq" means a long-drawn wail.

Some have said that "Zafir" (زفير) is a sound that rumbles in the throat, while "Shahiq" is a sound that moves back and forth in the chest; in any case, it refers to terrifying and distressing noises.

Then, to illustrate the intensity of Hell's rage, He adds: "It almost bursts with fury" (تَكادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ) [1]. Just like a massive vessel placed over an extremely high-heat fire, boiling and churning so violently that it is feared it might disintegrate at any moment, or like a person so enraged that they boil, roar, scream, and are on the verge of exploding. Yes, such is the scene of Hell, this center of Allah’s wrath!

Then He continues: "Every time a group is thrown into it, its keepers ask them [out of wonder and reproach], 'Did there not come to you a warner?'" Why then have you fallen into this dark state?! (كُلَّما أُلْقِيَ فِيها فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها أَ لَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ).

[1]: "Tamayyuz" (تميز) means to disintegrate and be scattered, and it was originally "Tatamayyaz" (تتميز).

They cannot believe that a human being, consciously and having had a heavenly leader, would be caught in such a fate and choose such a place for themselves.

"They will say, 'Yes, a warner had come to us, but we denied and said, "Allah has not sent down anything. You are not but in great error"'" (قالُوا بَلى‌ قَدْ جاءَنا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنا وَ قُلْنا ما نَزَّلَ اللَّهُ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ كَبِيرٍ). Not only did we not believe them and did not listen to their life-giving message, but we rose in opposition, called these spiritual physicians "misguided," and drove them away.

Then, pointing to the primary cause of their misery and deviation, "They will say: 'If only we had been listening or reasoning, we would not be among the companions of the Blaze!'" (وَ قالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ).

Yes, "And they will admit their sin, so [it is] alienation for the companions of the Blaze" (فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ). In these verses, while describing the terrifying fate of the people of Hell, the primary cause of their misery is highlighted. It says: on one hand, Allah has given listening ears, intellect, and intelligence, and on the other hand, He sent His prophets with clear proofs. If these two are joined together, human salvation is guaranteed.

But when a person has ears but does not hear with them, has eyes and does not see, and has an intellect and does not think, even if all the Divine prophets and heavenly books come to him, they will have no effect!

It is narrated that a group in the presence of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله praised a certain Muslim. The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "How is the man's intellect?" (كيف عقل الرجل؟!)

They said: "O Messenger of Allah! We are asking about his effort and diligence in worship and various good deeds, and you ask about his intellect?"

He replied: "Indeed, the fool suffers more from his foolishness than the wickedness of the wicked. Tomorrow on the Day of Resurrection, servants will only be raised in degrees and attain proximity to their Lord according to their intellects!" (ان الاحمق يصيب بحمقه اعظم من فجور الفاجر، و انما يرتفع العباد غدا فى الدرجات، و ينالون الزلفى من ربهم على قدر عقولهم!) [1]

"Suhq" (سحق) originally means to grind or pulverize, and it is also used for old, worn-out clothes, but here it means being far from the mercy of Allah. Therefore, "فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ" means may the people of Hell be far from Allah’s mercy. Since Allah’s curse is coupled with its actual realization, this sentence is proof that this group is entirely excluded from Allah’s mercy.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 324.

Key Point: The High Value of Intellect and Reason

This is not the first time the Holy Quran points to the extraordinary value of intellect and reason, identifying the primary sin of the people of Hell and the main factor of their misery as the disabling of this Divine power. Rather, anyone familiar with the Quran knows that it has revealed the importance of this subject on various occasions. Despite the fabrications of those who consider religion a means of numbing brains and ignoring the commands of reason, Islam bases the foundation of knowing God, happiness, and salvation on intellect and reason. Its address everywhere is to "those of understanding" (أولو الألباب), "those of vision" (أولو الأبصار), thinkers, and scholars.

In Islamic sources, the number of narrations in this regard is beyond count. Interestingly, the famous book Al-Kafi, which is among the most reliable sources of Hadith, consists of several books, the first of which is titled "The Book of Intellect and Ignorance." Anyone who observes the narrations cited in this book will realize the depth of Islam's insight on this matter. We will suffice here with mentioning two narrations:

In a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام (in the same book), it is stated: "Gabriel descended to Adam and said: 'I am commanded to give you a choice between one of these three gifts, so that you may choose one and leave the others.' Adam asked: 'What are they?' Gabriel replied: 'Intellect (Aql), Modesty (Haya), and Religion (Din).'

Adam said: 'I choose Intellect.' Gabriel then said to Modesty and Religion: 'Leave him and go about your way.' They replied: 'We are commanded to be wherever Intellect is and never to separate from it!' Gabriel said: 'If that is the case, then fulfill your mission,' and then he ascended to heaven!" [1]

This is the most subtle expression possible regarding intellect and its relationship with modesty and religion. For if intellect is separated from religion, it is blown away by the slightest thing or led into deviation. As for "modesty," which prevents a person from committing foul acts and sins, it too is the fruit of the tree of knowledge, intellect, and reason.

This shows that Adam possessed a significant portion of intellect, for when given the choice between these three, he chose the higher stage—intellect—and in its shadow, he acquired both religion and modesty.

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever is rational has religion, and whoever has religion enters Paradise" (من كان عاقلا كان له دين، و من كان له دين دخل الجنة). (Therefore, Paradise is the place for the rational) [2].

Of course, "intellect" here means true knowledge and insight, not the devilish cunning seen in the world's tyrannical and oppressive politicians, which, according to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Resembles intellect, but it is not intellect!" (شبيهة بالعقل، و ليست بالعقل) [3].

[1], [2], and [3]: "Usul al-Kafi," as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 382.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (١٢)
Indeed for those who fear their Lord in secret there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (12)
وَأَسِرُّوا۟ قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ ٱجْهَرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٣)
Speak secretly, or do so openly, indeed He knows well what is in the breasts. (13)
أَلَا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٤)
Would He who has created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware. (14)

Commentary

Is the Creator of the Universe Not Aware of the Secrets of the Universe?

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the disbelievers and their fate on the Day of Resurrection, the Quran, in the verses under discussion, turns to the believers and their magnificent rewards.

First, It states: "Indeed, those who fear their Lord in the unseen

will have forgiveness and a great reward" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ).

The expression "the unseen" (al-ghayb) here may refer to the knowledge of the unseen God, or the unseen Hereafter, or all of these combined.

Another possibility suggested in the interpretation of this verse is that it refers to fearing Allah regarding hidden sins; for if a person does not commit a hidden sin, they are even more likely to avoid open sin. Alternatively, this expression may refer to the station of sincerity of intention in abstaining from sins and fulfilling Divine commands, as a hidden action is further removed from ostentation (riya).

There is no obstacle to combining these interpretations.

The use of "forgiveness" (maghfirah) and "great reward" (ajrun kabir) in the indefinite form (nakirah) points to their magnificence and importance; that is, this forgiveness and reward are so great that they remain unknown to everyone.

Then, for emphasis, He adds: "And whether you conceal your speech or publicize it, indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts" (وَ أَسِرُّوا قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ اجْهَرُوا بِهِ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

Some commentators have narrated a reason for the revelation (shan al-nuzul) of this verse from Ibn Abbas, stating that a group of disbelievers or hypocrites were speaking ill of the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله behind his back, and Gabriel would inform the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Some of them said to one another:

"Conceal your speech (أَسِرُّوا قَوْلَكُمْ) so that the God of Muhammad does not hear!" The above verse was revealed and declared: whether you speak openly or in secret, Allah is aware of it [1].

[1]: Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 3, p. 66, and Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 86, under the verses discussed.

The next verse is, in fact, a proof for what was mentioned in the previous verse, stating: Does the One who created all beings not know their state?

While He is aware of the most precise secrets and is All-Knowing of everything (أَ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَ هُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ).

Various possibilities have been suggested in the interpretation of the phrase "Does He not know, He who created?" (أَ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ):

Some have said it means: Does the God who created the hearts not know the secrets within them?

Or: Does the God who created the servants remain unaware of the servants' secrets?

Or: Does the God who created the entire universe know all the secrets of the world, yet the secrets of man—who is a creature within this vast creation—remain hidden from Him?! In any case, to understand this reality, we must pay attention to the point that Allah's creation is continuous; that is, the grace of existence reaches the creatures from His side moment by moment, and it is not as if He created them and left them to themselves.

Fundamentally, contingent beings are dependent on His existence, and if their connection to His Pure Essence were severed for a single moment, they would head toward non-existence. Attention to this permanent connection and continuous creation is the best proof of Allah's knowledge regarding the secrets of all beings at every time and in every place.

Describing Allah as "the Subtle" (al-Latif) is because Latif is derived from the root lutf, meaning any precise or delicate matter, as well as any kind of rapid movement or subtle body. Therefore, Allah being Latif refers to His knowledge of the precise and delicate secrets

of creation, and sometimes it refers to the creation of subtle, small, microscopic, and sub-microscopic bodies.

This indicates that no matter how hidden your heart's intentions may be, or if you speak your words in private gatherings, or perform wrongful acts in seclusion, Allah, the Subtle, is aware of them all.

Some commentators, in interpreting "al-Latif," have said: (هو الذى يكلف اليسير و يعطى الكثير): "He is the One who mandates the easy and grants the abundant."

In reality, this too is a kind of subtlety and precision in mercy.

Others have said that describing Allah as Latif is because He permeates the interior of all things, and no place in the entire universe is empty of Him.

However, all of these return to one reality and serve as an emphasis on the depth of Allah's awareness and His knowledge of secrets, both hidden and manifest.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ ذَلُولًا فَٱمْشُوا۟ فِى مَنَاكِبِهَا وَكُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِهِۦ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلنُّشُورُ (١٥)
It is He who made the earth tractable for you; so walk on its flanks and eat of His provision, and towards Him is the resurrection. (15)
ءَأَمِنتُم مَّن فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَمُورُ (١٦)
Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not make the earth swallow you while it quakes? (16)
أَمْ أَمِنتُم مَّن فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَن يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ (١٧)
Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not unleash upon you a rain of stones? Soon you will know how My warning has been! (17)
وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (١٨)
Certainly those who were before them had impugned [My apostles]; but then how was My rebuttal! (18)

Commentary

No Criminal is Safe from His Punishment!

Following the discussions about the inhabitants of Hell and Paradise, and the believers and disbelievers in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion mention a portion of Divine blessings and then a part of His punishments to encourage and motivate joining the ranks of the people of Paradise and to warn against the path and ways of the people of Hell.

He says: "He is the One who made the earth submissive for you" (هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولًا).

"So walk among its slopes and shoulders, and eat of His provision, and know that unto Him is the resurrection" (فَامْشُوا فِي مَناكِبِها وَ كُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِهِ وَ إِلَيْهِ النُّشُورُ).

"Dhalul" (ذلول), meaning "submissive" or "tame," is the most comprehensive expression that can be used regarding the earth.

For this swift-moving mount, despite its multiple and extremely rapid movements, appears so calm that it seems as if it is absolutely stationary. Some scientists say the earth has fourteen different types of movement, three of which are its rotation around its axis, its revolution around the sun, and its movement along with the solar system within the galaxy. These movements, which possess great speed, are so smooth and gentle that until definitive proofs for the earth's motion were established, no one believed there was any movement at all! On the other hand, the earth's crust is neither so hard and harsh that it is uninhabitable, nor so loose and soft that it offers no stability; rather, it is perfectly submissive for human life. If, for example, most of the earth's surface were marshes where everything sank, or soft sands in which a person's leg would sink up to the knee, or sharp and jagged rocks that would bruise and wound the human body with a little walking, then the meaning of the earth's "unrest" would become clear.

Thirdly, its distance from the sun is not so small that everything burns from intense heat, nor so far that everything freezes from the cold. The atmospheric pressure on the globe is such that it ensures human comfort—neither so high that it suffocates him, nor so low that he disintegrates. The earth's gravity is not so strong that it crushes bones, nor so weak that a person is detached from the ground and flung into space with a single movement! In short, from every perspective, it is "Dhalul," submissive, and subjugated to human command. Interestingly, after describing the earth as submissive, He commands to walk upon its "Manakib" (shoulders). We know that "Manakib" is the plural of "Mankib," meaning shoulder. It is as if man sets foot on the shoulder of the earth, and the earth is so calm that he can maintain his balance.

At the same time, it is an indication that unless you take a step and make an effort, you will not benefit from the provisions of the earth! The term "Rizq" (provision) is also a very comprehensive term that includes all food substances of the earth, whether animal, vegetable, or mineral.

But know that these are not the ultimate goal of your creation; these are all means on the path toward "Nushur" (Resurrection) and your eternal life.

Following this encouragement and glad tidings, He turns to threats and warnings, adding:

"Do you feel secure from Him who is in the heaven, that He will not cause the earth to swallow you, and then it begins to sway [violently]?" (أَ أَمِنْتُمْ مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ الْأَرْضَ فَإِذا هِيَ تَمُورُ).

Yes, if He commands, this submissive and calm earth will rebel and turn into a wild animal; earthquakes will begin, cracks will appear in the ground, swallowing you and your houses and cities, and it will continue its shaking and agitation.

The phrase "Fa-idha hiya tamur" (فَإِذا هِيَ تَمُورُ - and then it sways/moves continuously) might be an indication that Allah can command the earth to swallow you and continuously move you from one point to another within its depths, such that even your grave will not be at rest! Or that the earth loses its tranquility forever, and a permanent earthquake governs it. Understanding this meaning is very easy for those who have lived in earthquake-prone areas and have seen that sometimes the earth has absolutely no rest for several days and nights, depriving everyone of sleep, food, and comfort; however, it is difficult for most of us who are accustomed to the earth's tranquility.

The expression "Man fi al-sama'" (مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ - He who is in the heaven) refers to the Pure Essence of Allah, whose sovereignty over all the heavens—let alone the earth—is certain. Some have also said it refers to the angels of Allah who are in the heavens and are commissioned to execute His command.

Then He adds: "It is not necessary for earthquakes to come to you; rather, He can give this command to the whirlwinds." "Or do you feel secure from Him who is in the heaven, that He will not send against you a storm of stones?" and bury you under a mountain of them?! (أَمْ أَمِنْتُمْ مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ أَنْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حاصِباً).

"And you shall soon know how [terrible] was My warning!" (فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ).

Understanding this meaning is also very simple for those who have seen shifting sands and "Hasib" (حاصب) winds (winds that move piles of pebbles and transfer them from one place to another). They know that in a few moments, houses or villages can be buried under a pile of shifting pebbles and sands, or caravans moving in the middle of the desert can be buried under them!

In fact, the above verses indicate that their punishment is not limited to the punishment of the Hereafter; in this world too, Allah can end their lives with a slight tremor of the earth or the movement of winds, and the best proof of this possibility is its occurrence among previous nations.

Therefore, in the last verse under discussion, He says: "And certainly those before them denied [the Divine signs and His messengers], then how [terrible] was My rejection [of them]!" (وَ لَقَدْ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ) [1]. We punished some with devastating earthquakes, some with lightning bolts, and some with floods or whirlwinds, and we left their ruined and silent cities as a lesson for others.

[1]: "Nakir" (نكير) means "denial" or "rejection," and here it is an allusion to punishment, because Allah's denial of the actions of these nations took place through their punishment. It should be noted that this word was originally "Nakiri," just as "Nadhir" in the previous verse was "Nadhiri"; the first-person pronoun "Ya" has been omitted, and the Kasra that indicates it remains.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ ۚ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍۭ بَصِيرٌ (١٩)
Have they not regarded the birds above them spreading and closing their wings? No one sustains them except the All-beneficent. Indeed, He watches all things. (19)
أَمَّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ جُندٌ لَّكُمْ يَنصُرُكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِلَّا فِى غُرُورٍ (٢٠)
Who is it that is your host who may help you, besides the All-beneficent? The faithless only dwell in delusion. (20)
أَمَّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُۥ ۚ بَل لَّجُّوا۟ فِى عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ (٢١)
Who is it that may provide for you if He withholds His provision? Indeed, they persist in defiance and aversion. (21)

Commentary

Look at These Birds Above You

In the opening verses of this Surah, when discussing the power and sovereignty of Allah, mention was made of the seven heavens and their stars and planets. However, in the first verse under discussion, this same issue of power is pursued by mentioning one of the seemingly small creatures of the universe. It says:

"Have they not seen the birds above them, spreading their wings and [sometimes] folding them?!" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صافَّاتٍ وَ يَقْبِضْنَ) [^1] These heavy bodies rise from the earth contrary to the law of gravity, and with complete ease, they continue their swift and smooth movement across the sky for hours, and sometimes weeks and months! one after another, without facing any difficulty.

Some, while flying, mostly keep their wings spread (صافات), as if a mysterious force is moving them.

While others constantly flap their wings (it is possible that يقبضن refers to this meaning).

Others sometimes flap and sometimes glide with spread wings.

A fourth group flaps for a while, and once they gain speed, they fold their wings completely and dive into the ocean of air (like sparrows!). In short, while they all fly, each has its own specific program.

Who created their bodies in such a way that they travel easily through the air?! And who bestowed this power upon their wings and taught them the science of flight? Especially in the complex collective flights of migratory birds that sometimes last for several months, covering thousands of kilometers, passing over many countries, mountains, valleys, forests, and seas to reach their destination—truly, who has given them all this strength and awareness?! Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "None holds them up [in the sky] except the Most Merciful (al-Rahman); indeed, He is Seeing of all things" and knows the needs of every creature (ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمنُ إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ بَصِيرٌ).

It is He who has provided them with various means and forces for flight. Yes, Allah the Most Merciful, whose general mercy encompasses all beings, has also granted the birds what they needed. The One who holds the birds in the sky is the same One who maintains the earth and other creatures, and whenever He wills, neither does the bird have the power to fly, nor does the earth maintain its stability.

The expression "صافات و يقبضن" (spreading and folding the wings) might refer to different types of birds, or to the different states of a single bird. [^2] Regarding the wonders of the world of birds and the marvels of their flight, we have discussed this in detail in Volume 11 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 338 to 341 (under verse 79 of Surah An-Nahl).

[^1]: "Al-Tayr" (الطير) is the plural of "Ta'ir" (طائر), and therefore its verb and description appear in plural form. The notion held by some that "Tayr" is singular contradicts the explicit statements of lexicographers. [^2]: The fact that "Saffat" (صافات) appears as an adjective and "Yaqbidna" (يقبضن) as a present-tense verb may be because the spreading of wings is a steady state, whereas the opening and closing (folding) of wings is a repetitive action (reflect on this).

In the next verse, it points to the fact that the disbelievers have no helper or assistant against the power of Allah. It says: "Who is it that could be an army for you to aid you against the Most Merciful?!" (أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ جُنْدٌ لَكُمْ يَنْصُرُكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ الرَّحْمنِ) [^1] Not only can they not help you in times of trouble, but if He wills, He can command those very things to punish and destroy you. Are not water, wind, soil, and fire your servants and the pillars of your life? Yet Allah commissioned these very elements to destroy rebellious nations. History also remembers many instances where those closest to kings, Pharaohs, and tyrants became the agents of their death. In contemporary history, it has often been seen that the most loyal forces of governments rose against them and issued their death warrants! "But the disbelievers are only in delusion" (إِنِ الْكافِرُونَ إِلَّا فِي غُرُورٍ). Veils of pride and ignorance have fallen over their intellects, not allowing them to see all these lessons of warning on the pages of history or in the corners of their own lives.

"Jund" (جند) originally means uneven and hard ground where many stones are gathered, and for this reason, a large army is also called "Jund."

Some commentators consider "Jund" in the verse under discussion to refer to idols, which will never be able to help the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection, but it appears that the verse has a broad meaning of which idols are just one instance.

[^1]: "Am" (أَمْ) in this sentence is a conjunction, "Man" (مَنْ) is the subject (mubtada), "Hadha" (هذا) is the second subject, "Al-ladhi" (الذي) is its predicate (khabar), and "Huwa jundun lakum" (هُوَ جُنْدٌ لَكُمْ) is its relative clause (silah), and "Yansurukum" (ينصركم) is a description of "Jund," and the entire phrase is the predicate of the first subject (Al-Bayan fi Gharib I'rab al-Qur'an, Vol. 2, p. 459). However, it is more appropriate that "Al-ladhi" be an appositive (atf-e bayan) and "Yansurukum" be the predicate, as the sentence is incomplete without it (reflect on this).

Again, for further emphasis, it adds: "Or who is it that can provide for you if He should withhold His provision?!" (أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُ) [^1] If He commands the sky not to rain, and the lands not to grow any plants, or if various plant pests destroy the crops, who has the ability to provide food for you? Or if He cuts off spiritual provisions and heavenly revelation from you, who has the ability to guide you?

These are obvious truths, but stubbornness and obstinacy are veils before human perception and intellect; therefore, at the end of the verse, it says: "But they persist in rebellion and aversion [from the truth]" (بَلْ لَجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَ نُفُورٍ).

Even today, with all the progress made in various aspects of human life, especially in the food industry, if it absolutely did not rain for just one year, what a catastrophe would occur throughout the world? Or if swarms of locusts and plant pests covered everywhere, what a tragedy would take place?

Key Points Four Factors for Human Failure

In the previous verses, we read that lacking a listening ear and an awake intellect is the most important factor that draws the people of Hell into the Fire. In the verses under discussion, we read that four other factors—namely "pride," "stubbornness," "rebellion" (utuw), and "aversion from the truth" (nufur)—cause human misery and misguidance.

If we look closely, we see that these factors are also related to the previous factor, because these ugly traits cast a veil over the ears and eyes of human insight, preventing one from perceiving the truths.

[^1]: The result of the conditional statement (jaza' al-shart) is omitted in the verse, and its estimation is: "If He withholds His provision, who will provide for you other than Him?"

Ayahs

أَفَمَن يَمْشِى مُكِبًّا عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦٓ أَهْدَىٰٓ أَمَّن يَمْشِى سَوِيًّا عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٢٢)
Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path? (22)
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَكُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۖ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (٢٣)
Say, ‘It is He who created you, and made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts. Little do you thank.’ (23)
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلَّذِى ذَرَأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٢٤)
Say, ‘It is He who created you on the earth, and toward Him you will be mustered.’ (24)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٥)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (25)
قُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْعِلْمُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَإِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢٦)
Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah; I am only a manifest warner.’ (26)
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ زُلْفَةً سِيٓـَٔتْ وُجُوهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَقِيلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَدَّعُونَ (٢٧)
When they see it brought near, the countenances of the faithless will be contorted, and [they will be] told, ‘This is what you were asking for!’ (27)

Commentary

The Upright Walkers on the Path of Monotheism

Following the previous verses concerning the disbelievers and the believers, the first verse under discussion reflects the state of these two groups through a striking parable, stating:

"Is he who walks prone upon his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path?" (أَ فَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى‌ وَجْهِهِ أَهْدى‌ أَمَّنْ يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ).

Here, the faithless individuals and the stubborn, arrogant oppressors are likened to someone traversing an uneven and winding road while fallen on his face, moving on his hands and feet or his chest. He neither sees the way correctly, nor is he able to control himself; he is unaware of obstacles and possesses no speed. He moves a little and becomes helpless.

In contrast, He likens the believers to upright individuals who advance easily along a smooth, level, and straight road with full speed, power, and awareness.

What a beautiful and precise analogy! Its effects are completely manifest in the lives of these two groups, and we witness them with our own eyes.

Some have considered the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and Abu Jahl to be the manifestations of these two groups; of course, these are clear instances of the above verse, but they do not limit the generality of the verse's concept.

Regarding the interpretation of "مُكِبًّا عَلى‌ وَجْهِهِ" (prone upon his face), several possibilities have been mentioned:

The one most consistent with its lexical meaning is what we mentioned above: one who has fallen on his face and moves forward on his hands, feet, and chest.

However, others have said: it means one who walks standing but with his head cast down, not observing his path at all.

Meanwhile, others believe it refers to someone who cannot maintain his balance; he takes a few steps forward, falls to the ground, and gets up, and this situation repeats continuously.

From some of the words of Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it also appears to mean someone whose entire attention is on his own condition and is oblivious to anything other than himself. However, the first meaning seems more appropriate due to the contrast with the state of the believers, which is described as "سويًّا" (upright/balanced).

In any case, is this the state of the disbeliever and the believer in the Hereafter, or in both worlds?

There is no evidence to limit the meaning of the verse; they are like this both in the life of this world and in the Hereafter.

Indeed, because faithless individuals are selfish, self-worshipping, and stubborn, seeing nothing but their own material and fleeting interests—and since their path is the path of following whims—they are like someone crawling through a rocky terrain on their chest, hands, and feet. But those who, in the light of faith, have been delivered from the shackles of carnal desires, possess deep insight and a smooth, clear path.

In the next verse, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He adds: "Say: It is He who created you and made for you hearing and vision and hearts; little is it that you give thanks" (قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ وَ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَ الْأَبْصارَ وَ الْأَفْئِدَةَ قَلِيلًا ما تَشْكُرُونَ).

Allah has placed at your disposal the means of observation and experience (vision), the means of becoming aware of the results of others' thoughts (hearing), and the means of reflecting on intellectual sciences (the heart). In short, He has provided you with all the necessary tools for acquiring both intellectual and traditional knowledge. Yet, few people offer thanks for all these great blessings, for the gratitude of a blessing is to utilize every blessing in the direction of the purpose for which it was created. Do all people use their eyes, ears, and intellect correctly in this way?!

Once again addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "Say: It is He who has multiplied you throughout the earth, and to Him you shall be gathered" (قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ).

In reality, the first verse specifies the "path," the second verse the "tools and means of work," and this verse the "goal and destination." That is: walk upon the right way and the straight path of Islamic faith, utilize all the tools of cognition, and move toward eternal life.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse, the expression "أنشأكم" (brought you into being) was used, and in this verse "ذرأكم" (multiplied/scattered you). The difference between these two expressions might be that the first sentence refers to the creation of man from non-existence (meaning you were nothing and He created you), and the second sentence refers to the creation of man from earthly matter, meaning We created you from the soil.

Then, in this same connection, He addresses the speech of the deniers of the Resurrection and the response to them, saying: "And they say: 'When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ مَتى‌ هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ).

Why do you not determine its exact date? Why do you not clarify everyone's duty in this regard?

Regarding what is meant by "هذَا الْوَعْدُ" (this promise), two possibilities have been mentioned:

First, the promise of the Resurrection; second, the promise of various worldly punishments, such as earthquakes, lightning bolts, and storms. However, considering the previous verse, the first meaning seems more appropriate, although combining both meanings is also possible.

In the next verse, He answers them thus: "Say: The knowledge is only with Allah, and I am only a clear warner" (قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ إِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ).

This expression is exactly like what has appeared in several verses of the Quran, including verse 187 of Al-A'raf: (قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي) "Say: The knowledge thereof is only with my Lord."

And it must be so, because if the date of the Resurrection were known, whenever it was a long distance away, people would fall into negligence, and if the distance were short, they would fall into a state resembling desperation. In either case, the educational goals would remain incomplete.

In the final verse under discussion, He adds: "But when they see it approaching, the faces of those who disbelieve will be distressed and blackened," such that signs of grief and sorrow will pour from them (فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ زُلْفَةً سِيئَتْ وُجُوهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) "and it will be said: 'This is that which you used to call for!'" (وَ قِيلَ هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَدَّعُونَ).

"تدعون" is from the root "دعاء" (supplication/calling), meaning you were constantly praying and requesting for the Resurrection to occur; now it has occurred, and there is no escape from it. [^1] This content is actually similar to what appears in verse 14 of Adh-Dhariyat addressed to the disbelievers, where it is said to them on the Day of Resurrection: (هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ) "This is that which you used to hasten." In any case, it refers to the punishment of the Resurrection, as most commentators have stated, and it serves as evidence that the sentence "مَتى‌ هذَا الْوَعْدُ" also refers to the promise of the Resurrection.

Hakim Abu al-Qasim Haskani says: When the disbelievers witnessed the high stations of Ali عليه السلام before Allah, their faces turned black (out of intense rage and anger). [^2] In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, this same meaning is narrated, stating that this verse was revealed concerning Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام and his companions. [^1] Of course, this interpretation, which has been narrated through both Shi'a and Sunni channels, is a type of "application" (tatbiq); otherwise, the subject of the verse pertains to the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, and such applications are not uncommon in the language of the narrations.

[^1]: "تدعون" is from the form of "Ifti'al" and from the root du'a, meaning to request, or from the root da'wa, meaning to request or to claim/deny something.

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 330.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 385.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَهْلَكَنِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَمَن مَّعِىَ أَوْ رَحِمَنَا فَمَن يُجِيرُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٨)
Say, ‘Tell me, whether Allah destroys me and those who are with me, or He has mercy on us, who will shelter the faithless from a painful punishment?’ (28)
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ وَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٩)
Say, ‘He is the All-beneficent; we have faith in Him, and in Him do we trust. Soon you will know who is in manifest error.’ (29)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَصْبَحَ مَآؤُكُمْ غَوْرًا فَمَن يَأْتِيكُم بِمَآءٍ مَّعِينٍۭ (٣٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, should your water sink down [into the ground], who will bring you running water?’ (30)

Commentary

Who Provides You with Running Water?

The above verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Mulk and all begin with the word "Say" (قُلْ) addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), are a continuation of the discussions held with the disbelievers in the preceding verses, reflecting other dimensions of those arguments.

First, addressing those who often awaited the death of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his companions, imagining that with his passing his religion would be dismantled and everything would end (a common expectation that defeated enemies hold regarding righteous leaders), He says: "Say, 'Have you considered: whether Allah should cause me to perish and those with me or have mercy upon us, who can protect the disbelievers from a painful punishment?'" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَهْلَكَنِيَ اللَّهُ وَمَنْ مَعِيَ أَوْ رَحِمَنَا فَمَنْ يُجِيرُ الْكَافِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the disbelievers of Mecca used to curse the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Muslims, praying for his death in the belief that if he passed away, his call would also cease. The above verse was revealed to answer them.

A similar meaning appears in verse 30 of Surah al-Tur, where it says: (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شاعِرٌ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ): "Or do they say, '[He is] a poet for whom we await a misfortune of time [death]!'" They are heedless of the fact that Mustafa (the Chosen One) has been promised by Divine grace that even if he dies, the Name of Truth shall not die. Indeed, he has been promised the victory of this religion and its dominion over the entire world, and the life or death of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) changes nothing.

Some have mentioned another interpretation for this verse, stating that it tells the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): Say, "We, having faith in Allah, are between fear and hope; what do you think of yourselves?!" However, the first interpretation seems more accurate.

Continuing this discourse, He adds: "Say, 'He is the Most Merciful; we have believed in Him, and upon Him we have relied. And you will [soon] know who it is that is in clear error.'" (قُلْ هُوَ الرَّحْمَنُ آمَنَّا بِهِ وَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُبِينٍ).

That is to say: if we have believed in Allah and chosen Him as our Guardian, Trustee, and Protector, the reason is clear: He is the Compassionate God (al-Rahman). His general mercy has reached everywhere, and the flow of His bounty has encompassed both friend and foe. A brief look at the universe and the pages of life bears witness to this claim. But what have your idols done?! Although your deviation and error are apparent even here, they will become clearer to you in the Hereafter, or in this world when Islam, with Divine assistance, triumphs over the army of disbelief—a miraculous victory where the truth will become even more evident.

This verse is, in fact, a form of consolation to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers, so they do not imagine they are alone in this vast struggle between truth and falsehood; rather, the All-Merciful and Compassionate Allah is their Helper and Supporter.

In the final verse, as a mention of one instance of Allah's general mercy of which many people are heedless, He says: "Say, 'Have you considered: if your water was to become sunken [into the earth], then who could bring you flowing water?'" (قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَصْبَحَ مَاؤُكُمْ غَوْراً فَمَنْ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِمَاءٍ مَعِينٍ).

We know that the earth is composed of two different layers: a "permeable layer" that absorbs water into itself, and beneath it an "impermeable layer" that holds the water there. All springs, wells, and subterranean canals (qanats) exist by the blessing of this specific composition. For if the entire earth were permeable to great depths, the waters would sink so far into the ground that no one could ever reach them. And if it were all impermeable, the waters would remain on the surface, turning into a swamp or quickly flowing into the seas, and thus all underground water reserves would be lost.

This is a small example of Allah's general mercy, with which human life and death are strictly intertwined.

"Ma'in" (مَعِين) is derived from the root "ma'n" (on the pattern of 'ta'n'), meaning the flow of water. Some have said it is taken from "ayn" (eye/spring) and the 'm' is extra; thus, some commentators have interpreted "ma'in" as water that can be seen with the eye, even if it is not flowing.

However, most have interpreted it as "flowing water." Although drinking water is not limited to flowing water, flowing waters are undoubtedly considered the best kind, whether in the form of springs, rivers, qanats, or gushing wells.

Some commentators have narrated that when one of the disbelievers heard this verse saying, "If your water was to become sunken into the earth, who could provide you with flowing water?" he said: "Strong men and sharp pickaxes! They will pull the water out from the depths of the earth!" But that night he slept, and a black fluid covered his eyes. In that state, he heard a voice saying: "Bring those strong men and sharp pickaxes to take this fluid out of your eyes!" Today we know that if the "impermeable layer" of the earth were to vanish, no strong-handed man and no sharp pickaxe could extract any water [1].

Key Point

In the narrations that have reached us from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), the final verse has been interpreted as the reappearance of Imam al-Mahdi (عليه السلام) and his world-spreading justice. Among these is a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) regarding the interpretation of this verse:

"It was revealed concerning the Imam who rises (al-Qa'im) (عليه السلام). He says: 'If your Imam becomes hidden from you, and you do not know where he is, who then will bring you a manifest Imam who will bring you the news of the heavens and the earth, and the lawful and prohibited of Allah?' Then he said: 'By Allah, the fulfillment (ta'wil) of this verse has not yet come, but it must surely come.'" [1]

Narrations in this regard are numerous, and it must be noted that they are all by way of "application" (tatbiq). In other words, the outward (zahir) meaning of the verse relates to flowing water, which is the source of life for living beings, and the inward (batin) meaning relates to the existence of the Imam and his world-encompassing knowledge and justice, which is also the source of life for human society.

We have said many times that the verses of the Quran have multiple meanings, both outward and inward. However, we also emphatically repeat that uncovering the inward depths of the verses is not possible except for the Prophet and the Infallible Imams, and no one has the right to propose something as the inward meaning of a verse on their own. What we say pertains to the outward meanings of the verses, and what pertains to the inward depths must only be heard from the Infallibles.

Surah al-Mulk began with the sovereignty and ownership of Allah, and it ends with His Mercy (Rahmaniyyah), which is also a branch of His sovereignty and ownership. Thus, its beginning and end are perfectly integrated.

O Allah! Include us in Your general and special mercy, and quench our thirst with the water of life from the guardianship (wilayah) of Your chosen servants.

O Lord! Hasten the reappearance of Imam al-Mahdi (عليه السلام), that fountain of the water of life, and quench the thirst of those longing for his beauty with his advent.

O God! You have granted us seeing eyes, hearing ears, and a wise intellect; remove the veils of selfishness and pride from before them, so that we may see the face of truth as it is, and walk upright on the Straight Path of Your guidance.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. "End of Surah al-Mulk" 12 Shawwal 1406 (June 20, 1986)
